channel.title: Sichuan on zhouyang — The 25th Frame
channel.description: Recent content in Sichuan on zhouyang — The 25th Frame

item.title: Suan Cai Yu: The Fish That Taught China to Love Sour
item.description:
The bowl is the size of a washbasin and the steam rising off it smells like a vinegar factory caught fire.

You’re in Chengdu. It’s 9pm on a Wednesday and the restaurant has exactly three walls, eight tables, and a line of people waiting on plastic stools that stretches halfway down the block. Every table has the same thing: a massive ceramic bowl of Suan Cai Yu (酸菜鱼, suān cài yú), the surface shimmering with chili oil, green Sichuan peppercorns bobbing like tiny planets, and strips of pickled mustard greens (酸菜, suān cài) tangled among fillets so tender they threaten to dissolve on contact with chopsticks.

item.title: Yu Xiang Rou Si: The Sichuan Dish Whose Name Has Lied to You Your Whole Life
item.description: The wok makes a sound like tearing silk. It’s the sound of shredded pork hitting oil at exactly the right temperature — hot enough to sear instantly, not so hot it smokes. A beat later the pickled chili paste goes in and the kitchen fills with something that smells like vinegar and caramel and fire all at once. You’re standing in a Chengdu apartment the size of a New York walk-in closet. The cook — someone’s grandmother, probably — moves faster than your eyes can track. Thirty seconds, start to finish. The plate lands on the table. The rice is already waiting.

item.title: How to Cook Kung Pao Chicken: The Real Way
item.description: Editor’s Note: Since launching this column, we’ve heard one request more than any other: the stories are great, but how do we actually cook these? Today we’re fixing that — six classic recipes, dropping at once. Here’s how to make 宫保鸡丁的正宗做法。 Read the story behind this dish: Kung Pao Chicken: The Stir-Fry That Made Sichuan Famous

item.title: How to Cook Real Mapo Tofu: The Sichuan Way
item.description: Editor’s Note: Since launching this column, we’ve heard one request more than any other: the stories are great, but how do we actually cook these? Today we’re fixing that — six classic recipes, dropping at once. Here’s how to make 麻婆豆腐的正宗做法。 This is the practical companion to Mapo Tofu: The Pockmarked Granny’s Revenge — read that first for the history, then come back here to cook.

item.title: How to Cook Sichuan Boiled Fish: The Chili Oil Bath
item.description: Editor’s Note: Since launching this column, we’ve heard one request more than any other: the stories are great, but how do we actually cook these? Today we’re fixing that — six classic recipes, dropping at once. Here’s how to make 水煮鱼的正宗做法。 This is the practical companion to Sichuan Boiled Fish: The Dish Whose Name Lied to You — read that first for the story, then come back here to cook.

item.title: How to Cook Twice-Cooked Pork: The Sichuan Way
item.description: Editor’s Note: Since launching this column, we’ve heard one request more than any other: the stories are great, but how do we actually cook these? Today we’re fixing that — six classic recipes, dropping at once. Here’s how to make 回锅肉的正宗做法。 Read the story behind this dish: Twice-Cooked Pork: The Sichuan Home Classic That Tests Every Cook

item.title: Sichuan Boiled Fish: The Dish Whose Name Lied to You
item.description: You walk into a restaurant on Yulin West Road in Chengdu and the first thing that hits you isn’t the noise or the heat. It’s the smell — hot rapeseed oil hitting a mound of dried chilies and Sichuan peppercorns, releasing something between toasted nuts and an electrical fire. The sound arrives half a second later: a violent sizzle that makes every table in the room look up.

item.title: Twice-Cooked Pork: The Sichuan Home Classic That Tests Every Cook
item.description: The slab of pork belly hits the wok of cold water with a soft thud. Ginger slices, a knot of scallion, a splash of Shaoxing wine. Your grandmother brings it to a boil, drops the flame to a whisper. Forty minutes. The kitchen fills with the clean, porky scent of simmering fat and ginger — not the aggressive smell of a stir-fry, but something patient. She lifts the pork out with chopsticks, sets it on the cutting board. The skin is pale, the fat layer translucent, wobbling slightly as it cools. You reach for a piece. She slaps your hand away without looking.

item.title: Dan Dan Noodles: The Street Snack That Outran Its Own City
item.description: It’s 11pm in Chengdu and the alley is mostly dark except for one cart with a single bulb swinging from a wire. The cart has two cabinets — one holds a charcoal stove, the other stacks of blue-rimmed bowls. The man behind it scoops a spoonful of sesame paste into a bowl, then chili oil the color of brake lights, then a splash of black vinegar. The smell hits you ten feet out: roasted huājiāo sizzling in rendered pork fat, fermented yácài releasing its funky-sweet depth, garlic crushed rough and still raw.

item.title: Mapo Tofu: The Pockmarked Granny's Revenge
item.description: The first time I ate real Mapo Tofu, I was nineteen years old, one year into my computer science degree at Fudan, and deeply suspicious of anything that made my face go numb. A Sichuan roommate dragged me to a hole-in-the-wall near Wujiaochang — the kind of place with plastic stools, grease-stained walls, and a line of taxi drivers out the door. “This is the test,” he said. “If you can finish the bowl, you’re allowed to date a Sichuan girl.”

item.title: Kung Pao Chicken: The Stir-Fry That Made Sichuan Famous
item.description:
The wok hits the gas burner and the whole kitchen snaps to attention.

You’re twelve, standing in a Sichuan alleyway restaurant the size of a shipping container. Your father just ordered in rapid-fire dialect and the cook, a man with forearms like bridge cables, is already moving — oil shimmering, garlic hitting steel, then the dried chilies go in and the air turns into pepper spray. Everyone within ten meters starts coughing. Your mother pulls a tissue from her sleeve and hands it to you without looking away from her tea.
